name: PgBouncer Connection Pooling Setup
description: Deploy PgBouncer as a connection pooler to reduce PostgreSQL connection overhead and support thousands of concurrent clients.
trendingReason: Connection pooling is essential for serverless and microservice architectures that create frequent short-lived connections
prerequisites: PgBouncer installed (apt install pgbouncer or equivalent)

steps.title: Configure PgBouncer databases
steps.description: Edit pgbouncer.ini to define which databases to pool and the upstream PostgreSQL connection.
steps.command:
cat > /etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini << 'INI'
[databases]
mydb = host=127.0.0.1 port=5432 dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
listen_addr = 0.0.0.0
listen_port = 6432
auth_type = scram-sha-256
auth_file = /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 1000
default_pool_size = 20
min_pool_size = 5
reserve_pool_size = 5
server_idle_timeout = 300
INI
steps.tip: Transaction pooling mode (pool_mode = transaction) gives the best connection reuse for most applications.

steps.title: Create the authentication file
steps.description: Add user credentials that PgBouncer uses to authenticate clients.
steps.tip: Generate the SCRAM hash from PostgreSQL: SELECT rolname, rolpassword FROM pg_authid WHERE rolname = 'app_user';

steps.title: Start PgBouncer
steps.description: Launch PgBouncer and verify it is listening for connections.
steps.command: sudo systemctl start pgbouncer && sudo systemctl status pgbouncer

steps.title: Connect through PgBouncer
steps.description: Connect to PostgreSQL through the PgBouncer proxy port.

steps.title: Monitor PgBouncer statistics
steps.description: Connect to the PgBouncer admin console to view pool statistics.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# PgBouncer Monitoring Script
set -euo pipefail

PGBOUNCER_HOST="127.0.0.1"
PGBOUNCER_PORT="6432"

echo "=== PgBouncer Status ==="
sudo systemctl status pgbouncer --no-pager | head -5

echo ""
echo "=== Connection Pools ==="
psql -h "$PGBOUNCER_HOST" -p "$PGBOUNCER_PORT" -U pgbouncer -d pgbouncer -c "SHOW POOLS;"

echo ""
echo "=== Active Clients ==="
psql -h "$PGBOUNCER_HOST" -p "$PGBOUNCER_PORT" -U pgbouncer -d pgbouncer -c "SHOW CLIENTS;" | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== Server Connections ==="
psql -h "$PGBOUNCER_HOST" -p "$PGBOUNCER_PORT" -U pgbouncer -d pgbouncer -c "SHOW SERVERS;" | head -20

echo ""
echo "=== Statistics ==="
psql -h "$PGBOUNCER_HOST" -p "$PGBOUNCER_PORT" -U pgbouncer -d pgbouncer -c "SHOW STATS;"

echo ""
echo "=== Configuration ==="
psql -h "$PGBOUNCER_HOST" -p "$PGBOUNCER_PORT" -U pgbouncer -d pgbouncer -c "SHOW CONFIG;" | grep -E "pool_mode|max_client_conn|default_pool_size"

faq.question: What pool mode should I use?
faq.answer: Transaction mode is recommended for most applications. It returns connections to the pool after each transaction, maximizing reuse. Session mode holds connections for the entire client session. Statement mode is for simple read-only workloads.

faq.question: What are the limitations of transaction pooling?
faq.answer: In transaction mode, session-level features like prepared statements, SET commands, LISTEN/NOTIFY, and advisory locks do not work reliably because different transactions may use different server connections.

faq.question: How do I size the connection pool?
faq.answer: Set default_pool_size to roughly 2-3x your CPU cores on the PostgreSQL server. For a 4-core server, start with 10-15 connections per pool. Monitor pg_stat_activity to see actual usage patterns.
